message.content: {"schema_version":"stella.literature_hvs_candidates.v0.1","generated_at":"2026-06-13T03:54:00","paper":{"arxiv_id":"2003.12766","bibcode":"2020A&A...637A..53K","title":"Hypervelocity Stars in the Gaia era. Revisiting the most extreme stars from the MMT survey","month":"2020-03","source_note_json":"notes/2020/2020-03/2020-03.json","links":{"abs":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.12766","pdf":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2003.12766"}},"inputs":{"paper_dir":"literature/2003.12766","audit_path":"literature/2003.12766/audit.json","catalog_review_path":"literature/2003.12766/catalog_review.json","catalog_extraction_path":"literature/2003.12766/catalog_extraction.json","ecsv_paths":["literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-atmos-results.ecsv","literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-B1085.ecsv","literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-hvs5.ecsv","literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-stellar_params.ecsv","literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-kinematic-AS.ecsv","literature/2003.12766/catalog_tables/table-tab-kinematic-TF.ecsv"]},"extraction":{"status":"candidates_found","extracted_at":"2026-06-13T03:54:00","extractor":"agent","summary":"The paper reanalyzes 40 stars from the MMT HVS survey using new spectroscopic models and Gaia DR2 astrometry. 37 stars have full 6D kinematics and are presented as hypervelocity star candidates with bound/unbound probabilities. The extraction includes all 37 kinematic sample stars as candidates possibly unbound from the Milky Way.","tooling":{"agent_runtime":"","model_id":"","prompt_version":"","request_parameters":{}}},"method_chain":[{"id":"step-01","depends_on":[],"step_type":"input_catalog","summary":"MMT HVS survey sample of 42 B-type stars from Brown et al. (2014), restricted to the 40 stars for which MMT spectra are available. Raw spectra are provided for analysis.","inputs":[],"outputs":["MMT raw spectra (40 stars)"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":99,"end_line":99,"context":"we extend the spectrophotometric analysis to 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers of the MMT HVS sample."}]},{"id":"step-02","depends_on":[],"step_type":"input_catalog","summary":"Multi-band photometry compiled from GALEX, Gaia DR2, SDSS DR12, Pan-STARRS1, SkyMapper DR1.1, BATC, VST-ATLAS-DR3, VST-KiDS-DR3, APASS-DR9, UKIDSS-DR9, and VISTA via VizieR.","inputs":[],"outputs":["multi-band photometry"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":272,"end_line":272,"context":"Photometric data were compiled from a variety of surveys covering the ultraviolet, the optical, and the infrared mainly using the VizieR catalog access tool."}]},{"id":"step-03","depends_on":[],"step_type":"astrometric_calibration","summary":"Proper motions from Gaia DR2 and HST/WFC3+ACS. The more precise measurement is adopted per star: HST for HVS1, HVS10, HVS12, HVS13; Gaia DR2 for B711; all others use Gaia DR2. RUWE < 1.4 verified for all.","inputs":[],"outputs":["adopted proper motions (37 stars)"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":288,"end_line":288,"context":"Proper motions were mainly taken from Gaia DR2. ... we chose the more precise measurement when Gaia as well as HST proper motions were available, which implies that we used HST data for HVS1, HVS10, HVS12, and HVS13 and Gaia DR2 data for B711."}]},{"id":"step-04","depends_on":["step-01","step-03"],"step_type":"sample_selection","summary":"From the 40 stars with spectra, 37 have complete astrometric data. HVS11, HVS14, HVS23 lack proper motions and are excluded. This step defines the kinematic sample of 37 stars.","inputs":["spectroscopic sample","proper motions"],"outputs":["kinematic sample (37 stars)"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":284,"end_line":284,"context":"proper motion measurements were not available for HVS11, HVS14, and HVS23, which is why the kinematic analysis encompasses only 37 out of the 40 stars in the sample."}]},{"id":"step-05","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"radial_velocity_measurement","summary":"Radial velocity obtained by χ² minimization fitting of flux-calibrated MMT spectra against the ADS synthetic grid.","inputs":["MMT flux-calibrated spectra"],"outputs":["radial_velocity"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":150,"end_line":150,"context":"four fitting parameters for the stellar spectrum: ... the radial velocity v_rad."}]},{"id":"step-06","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"stellar_parameter_inference","summary":"Effective temperature T_eff, surface gravity log(g), and projected rotational velocity v sin(i) derived by χ² minimization fitting of flux-calibrated MMT spectra against the ADS synthetic grid.","inputs":["MMT flux-calibrated spectra","model grid"],"outputs":["T_eff","log_g","v_sin_i"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":150,"end_line":150,"context":"four fitting parameters ... T_eff, log(g), v sin i, and v_rad."}]},{"id":"step-07","depends_on":[],"step_type":"other","summary":"Unused reserved step.","inputs":[],"outputs":[],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[]},{"id":"step-08","depends_on":["step-06"],"step_type":"stellar_parameter_inference","summary":"Derivation of stellar masses, ages, radii, luminosities, and Ω/Ω_crit from position in Kiel diagram compared to rotating MS evolutionary tracks (Georgy et al. 2013).","inputs":["T_eff","log_g","evolutionary tracks"],"outputs":["mass","age","radius","luminosity","Omega_over_Omega_crit"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":226,"end_line":226,"context":"stellar masses M, ages τ, radii R, luminosities L, and ratios ... Ω/Ω_crit can be derived by comparing the stars' position in the Kiel diagram with theoretical predictions."}]},{"id":"step-09","depends_on":[],"step_type":"solar_position_and_motion","summary":"Solar position and velocity assumptions for Galactocentric coordinate transformation. Paper states R0 = 8.3 kpc, Sun on negative x-axis. Other parameters (z0, v_circ, solar peculiar motion) are adopted from Irrgang et al. (2013) / Paper II but not explicitly reported.","inputs":[],"outputs":["solar Galactocentric position and velocity"],"parameters":[{"name":"R0","raw_value":"8.3","value":"8.3","unit":"kpc","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":325,"end_line":325,"context":"the solar radius (dashed; 8.3 kpc)"}]},{"name":"z0","raw_value":"not reported","value":"","description":"Not explicitly stated in this paper; adopted from Irrgang et al. (2013) via Paper II.","source_refs":[]},{"name":"v_circ_sun","raw_value":"not reported","value":"","description":"Not explicitly stated in this paper.","source_refs":[]},{"name":"solar_motion_u","raw_value":"not reported","value":"","description":"Not explicitly stated in this paper.","source_refs":[]},{"name":"solar_motion_v","raw_value":"not reported","value":"","description":"Not explicitly stated in this paper.","source_refs":[]},{"name":"solar_motion_w","raw_value":"not reported","value":"","description":"Not explicitly stated in this paper.","source_refs":[]}],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":307,"end_line":307,"context":"right-handed Cartesian Galactic coordinate system in which the Sun is located on the negative x-axis and the z-axis points to the Galactic north pole."},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":284,"end_line":284,"context":"The strategy of the kinematic analysis is identical to that of Paper II."}]},{"id":"step-10","depends_on":["step-03","step-05","step-18","step-09"],"step_type":"velocity_calculation","summary":"Construction of full 6D phase space: positions, proper motions, radial velocities, and distances combined to compute Galactocentric Cartesian positions (x,y,z) and velocities (vx,vy,vz). Galactic rest-frame velocity v_Grf = (vx²+vy²+vz²)^(1/2).","inputs":["RA,Dec","proper motions","radial velocities","distances","solar parameters"],"outputs":["galactocentric_x","galactocentric_y","galactocentric_z","galactocentric_vx","galactocentric_vy","galactocentric_vz","v_Grf"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":284,"end_line":284,"context":"Complementing our revised radial velocities and spectrophotometric distances with positions and proper motions ... gave us the full six-dimensional phase space information."}]},{"id":"step-11","depends_on":[],"step_type":"galactic_potential_model","summary":"Two Galactic mass models: Model I (revision of Allen & Santillan 1991) and Model II (Wilkinson & Evans 1999 flat rotation curve). Both calibrated with Gaia DR2 constraints. Used to compute local escape velocities and integrate orbits.","inputs":[],"outputs":["potential_models","escape_velocity"],"parameters":[{"name":"potential_name","raw_value":"Model I (Allen & Santillan revision)","value":"Model I (Allen & Santillan revision)","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":293,"end_line":293,"context":"Model~I is a revision of the popular model by Allen & Santillan (1991)"}]},{"name":"potential_name","raw_value":"Model II (Wilkinson & Evans flat rotation)","value":"Model II (Wilkinson & Evans flat rotation)","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":293,"end_line":293,"context":"Model~II is based on the flat rotation curve model by Wilkinson & Evans (1999)"}]}],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":291,"end_line":293,"context":"The trajectories of all targets were computed in two different Milky Way mass models."}]},{"id":"step-12","depends_on":["step-10","step-11","step-08"],"step_type":"orbit_integration","summary":"Monte Carlo orbit integration with 1.5 million realizations, backwards in time up to 15 Gyr. Correlated proper motion errors from Gaia DR2 included. Plane-crossing events within 99% confidence limit on stellar age are recorded. Flight times and disk-crossing parameters derived.","inputs":["6D phase space","potential models","stellar ages"],"outputs":["orbit_ensembles","plane_crossing_positions","plane_crossing_velocities","flight_time"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":284,"end_line":284,"context":"Propagation of uncertainties ... standard Monte Carlo approach with 1.5 million realizations."}]},{"id":"step-13","depends_on":["step-12","step-11"],"step_type":"escape_or_bound_assessment","summary":"Bound/unbound assessment: local escape velocity v_esc from potential model, compared to v_Grf. Bound probability P_b (%) from fraction of bound Monte Carlo realizations. Values tabulated for both Model I and Model II.","inputs":["v_Grf","potential model"],"outputs":["v_esc","v_Grf_minus_v_esc","P_b"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":307,"end_line":307,"context":"The Galactic rest-frame velocity v_Grf, the local Galactic escape velocity v_esc ... are listed."},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":440,"end_line":442,"context":"Whether a star is bound to the Galaxy or not is determined by the Galactic potential, in particular the mass of the Galactic dark matter halo."}]},{"id":"step-14","depends_on":["step-12","step-08"],"step_type":"origin_assessment","summary":"Classification of stars into origin categories: unconstrained, outer rim, disk origin. Ejection velocities v_ej,p computed at plane crossing, correcting for Galactic rotation. Flight times compared to stellar ages.","inputs":["plane_crossing_locations","stellar ages","plane_crossing_velocities"],"outputs":["origin_category","ejection_velocity","origin_site"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":329,"end_line":331,"context":"we group the stars in three categories based on their inferred spatial origin."},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":302,"end_line":302,"context":"the ejection velocity v_ej,p ... is a more useful quantity."}]},{"id":"step-15","depends_on":[],"step_type":"other","summary":"Unused reserved step.","inputs":[],"outputs":[],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[]},{"id":"step-16","depends_on":["step-02","step-06"],"step_type":"photometric_or_sed_modeling","summary":"SED fitting of multi-band photometry with synthetic SEDs (Atlas12 models fixed to spectroscopic T_eff and log g). Angular diameter Θ (scaling factor) and color excess E(B-V) derived via χ² minimization.","inputs":["multi-band photometry","T_eff","log_g"],"outputs":["angular_diameter","E_BminusV"],"parameters":[{"name":"other","raw_value":"R_V = 3.1","value":"3.1","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":276,"end_line":276,"context":"R_V fixed at 3.1, its typical value for the interstellar medium."}]}],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":276,"end_line":276,"context":"The resulting observed SEDs were compared to synthetic ones ... Only the angular diameter and the color excess were fitted."}]},{"id":"step-17","depends_on":["step-01"],"step_type":"quality_filter","summary":"Quality assessment of spectroscopic fitting: wavelength-averaged signal-to-noise ratio (S/N_tot), reduced χ² at best fit, and number of individual exposures per star, from Table tab:atmos-results.","inputs":["MMT spectra"],"outputs":["S_N_tot","reduced_chi2","N_exposures"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":144,"end_line":144,"context":"the average S/N of the co-added spectra is only of the order of 10--30."},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":110,"end_line":128,"context":"Table tab:atmos-results listing S/N_tot, χ²_red, and N."}]},{"id":"step-18","depends_on":["step-16","step-08"],"step_type":"distance_estimation","summary":"Spectrophotometric distances computed as d = 2R/Θ, using angular diameter from SED fitting and stellar radius from evolutionary models.","inputs":["angular_diameter","stellar_radius"],"outputs":["distance"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":280,"end_line":280,"context":"The resulting angular diameters Θ = 2 R / d were combined with the stellar radii R ... to calculate the spectrophotometric distances d."}]},{"id":"step-19","depends_on":["step-03"],"step_type":"quality_filter","summary":"Astrometric quality assessment using Gaia DR2 RUWE: all stars have RUWE < 1.4, confirming well-behaved astrometric solutions.","inputs":["Gaia DR2 proper motions"],"outputs":["RUWE"],"parameters":[],"source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":288,"end_line":288,"context":"For all objects in the sample, RUWE is below the recommended value of of 42 B-type stars with Galactic rest-frame radial velocity exceeding +275 km/s, selected from Brown et al. (2014).","decision":"partially_included","reason":"Of the 42 stars, 40 have MMT spectra analyzed in this paper; 37 have proper motions enabling full kinematic analysis and bound/unbound assessment. Two stars lack spectra; three (HVS11, HVS14, HVS23) lack proper motions.","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":99,"end_line":99,"context":"we extend the spectrophotometric analysis to 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers of the MMT HVS sample."}]},{"group_id":"kinematic_sample_37","description":"Stars with full 6D kinematics (positions, proper motions, radial velocities, spectrophotometric distances) for which bound/unbound probabilities, escape velocities, and origin assessments are presented.","decision":"included","reason":"The paper presents these 37 stars as HVS candidates with complete kinematic analysis, providing bound probabilities v_Grf - v_esc, and origin categories. They are the primary candidate set.","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":284,"end_line":284,"context":"the kinematic analysis encompasses only 37 out of the 40 stars in the sample."},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":307,"end_line":307,"context":"The detailed results of the kinematic analyses are listed in Tables tab:kinematic-AS and tab:kinematic-TF."}]},{"group_id":"alternative_scenarios","description":"Supplementary kinematic rows in Model I/II tables for HVS5 using Brown (2015) parameters, B576 under the BHB assumption (M=0.5 M_sun), and B598 under the ELM WD assumption (M=0.25 M_sun). These are alternative distance/mass scenarios for the same stars.","decision":"excluded","reason":"These rows are not independent candidates but different model assumptions for the same objects. The primary MS-based rows are used for the candidate list. The alternatives are discussed in the paper text for comparison.","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":396,"end_line":397,"context":"HVS5: we illustrate the impact of the different distance estimates on the outcome of the kinematic analysis."},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":420,"end_line":429,"context":"B576: Assuming a MS nature ... it would be the only star in the sample for which an origin in the Galactic disk could be ruled out ... However, the measured rotational velocity ... make it much more plausible that B576 is actually a low-mass BHB star."},{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":398,"end_line":418,"context":"B598: a puzzling object ... if B598 was a MS star, it would be one of our best candidates for the ejection by the Hills mechanism ... However, the star is likely an evolved low mass star of ~0.2 M_sun."}]},{"group_id":"missing_spectra","description":"Two stars from the original 42-star MMT sample for which MMT spectra were not available to the authors.","decision":"excluded","reason":"Without spectra, spectrophotometric analysis is impossible; these objects cannot be assessed as HVS candidates in this work.","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":99,"end_line":99,"context":"We did not have access to the spectra of the two missing object."}]},{"group_id":"missing_proper_motions","description":"Stars HVS11, HVS14, and HVS23 which have MMT spectra and derived atmospheric/stellar parameters but lack proper motion measurements from Gaia DR2 or HST.","decision":"excluded","reason":"Without proper motions, the 6D phase space is incomplete; Galactic rest-frame velocities cannot be computed and unbound status cannot be assessed.","source_refs":[{"kind":"text","path":"literature/2003.12766/arxiv_source/aa.tex","start_line":284,"end_line":284,"context":"proper motion measurements were not available for HVS11, HVS14, and HVS23, which is why the kinematic analysis encompasses only 37 out of the 40 stars in the sample."}]}]}
message.reasoning_content:
We need to fix the validation errors. The main issues are:
1. The method chain IDs are not in ascending order; they were placed out of order relative to the numeric IDs. The rule is: never renumber, reuse, or delete existing step ids; append new steps at the end with the next sequential id. Currently, the steps are listed as step-01, step-02, step-03, step-04, step-05, step-06, step-08, step-16, step-17, step-18, step-07, step-09, step-10, step-11, step-12, step-13, step-14, step-15, step-19. That's clearly not ascending; step-07 appears after step-18, etc. The error says step-07 breaks ascending order, so we need to reorder the method_chain array so that step ids appear in ascending order: step-01, step-02, ..., step-19. However, the error message says "never renumber or insert between existing steps; append new steps at the end". So we must preserve the step ids as given but reorder them in the array to be sequentially increasing. That should be fine: just sort them by id numerically. But careful: The validation says step-07 breaks ascending order because it appears after step-18? Actually, the array order is step-01 (index 0), step-02 (1), step-03 (2), step-04 (3), step-05 (4), step-06 (5), step-08 (6), step-16 (7), step-17 (8), step-18 (9), step-07 (10), step-09 (11), etc. The numeric order is not followed. We need to reorder the array so that the step ids appear in ascending order: step-01, step-02, step-03, step-04, step-05, step-06, step-07, step-08, step-09, step-10, step-11, step-12, step-13, step-14, step-15, step-16, step-17, step-18, step-19. That's just sorting by id. The rule says "never renumber... append new steps at the end". This is about adding new steps later, not about reordering. Reordering existing steps to be in ascending order is acceptable and necessary to fix the validation.

2. The candidate roster errors: The errors state that method step 'step-05' is used as direct producer for incompatible quantity categories ['radial_velocity', 'stellar_parameter']; split this method step. Similarly step-06 and step-07. However, note that in the scaffold stage, we are only providing identifier stubs; we are NOT including any quantity records yet. The validation errors about step-05/06/07 being used as direct producers in candidates must be because the validator is looking at the method_chain and inferring that these steps might be used as direct producers for multiple quantity categories incorrectly? Wait, the error says: "$.candidates: method step 'step-05' is used as direct producer for incompatible quantity categories ['radial_velocity', 'stellar_parameter']; split this method step". That suggests that somewhere in the candidates, we have already assigned method_refs that point to step-05 for both radial_velocity and stellar_parameters. But in our scaffold, we only filled identifiers, no other fields. So how can this error occur? Possibly the validator is checking the method_chain against some predefined mappings and sees that step-05 is defined with summary covering both radial velocity and stellar parameters, and that the step_type 'radial_velocity_measurement' is indeed the canonical type for radial velocity, but the step-05 also appears to be the direct producer for stellar parameters? Let's re-read the skill: For radial velocity, the direct producer should be `radial_velocity_measurement` or `input_catalog`; for stellar parameters, `stellar_parameter_inference`. In our method_chain, step-05 is `radial_velocity_measurement` and step-06 is `stellar_parameter_inference`. That seems correct. But the error says step-05 is used for incompatible categories. Could it be that the validator sees that step-05 is the only step that produces radial velocities, and perhaps some candidate in the roster has an implicit assignment that would require splitting? But no candidates have any quantities yet. So maybe the error is about the method_chain itself: step-05 is described as also deriving stellar parameters? In our summary, step-05 only mentions radial velocity. So why the error?

Maybe the validator is analyzing the method_chain independently, and because step-05 and step-06 both have the same source text line? No. The error might be that step-05 is used as a direct producer for multiple quantity categories within the method_chain's own dependency structure? But that's not relevant. Alternatively, maybe the error is that in the method_chain, we have step-05 with step_type 'radial_velocity_measurement' and step-06 with 'stellar_parameter_inference', but both steps have the same inputs and outputs? That might trigger a warning? But the error specifically says "method step 'step-05' is used as direct producer for incompatible quantity categories ['radial_velocity', 'stellar_parameter']; split this method step". This seems like a check that in the candidates section, if a step is referenced as method_refs for both radial_velocity and stellar_parameter, it's invalid because those are incompatible categories. But we haven't filled those fields. Could it be that the skeleton init script creates default method_refs? No, skeleton has empty method_refs. So why does the validator complain? Possibly the validator scans the method_chain summaries and infers that step-05, which mentions both radial velocity and stellar parameter fitting, is ambiguous. However, the summary says: "Radial velocities obtained from χ² minimization fitting... Simultaneously determined with stellar parameters in step-06 but modeled here as a separate direct producer for radial velocity."
